perturbation.original_text: 1.18 "Patent" means any United States or foreign patent or patent application, including any provisional application, continuation, continuation-in-part, divisional, registration, confirmation, revalidation, reissue, PCT application, patent term extension, supplementary protection certificate, and utility model, as well as all foreign counterparts of any of the foregoing, and related extensions or restorations of terms thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.18 "Patent" means any United States patent, excluding any provisional application, continuation, continuation-in-part, divisional, registration, confirmation, revalidation, reissue, PCT application, patent term extension, supplementary protection certificate, and utility model, and excluding all foreign counterparts of any of the foregoing, and related extensions or restorations of terms thereof.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the definition of "Patent" to only include United States patents and explicitly excluding provisional applications, continuations, continuations-in-part, divisional, registration, confirmation, revalidation, reissue, PCT application, patent term extension, supplementary protection certificate, and utility model, and excluding all foreign counterparts of any of the foregoing, and related extensions or restorations of terms thereof., this contradicts established legal definitions and interpretations of patent law, which include these items and foreign patents.
perturbation.law_explanation: 35 U.S.C. § 101 defines what can be patented and does not limit patents to only being regular United States patents. This artificially limits the scope of 'Patent' contrary to established patent law and introduces ambiguity. It would exclude patent applications (provisional, continuation, etc) as well as patents from other countries. This is a contradiction because patent rights extend beyond granted patents to pending applications.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 35 U.S. Code § 101 - Inventions patentable | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 101 - Inventions patentable U.S. Code Notes prev | next Whoever invents or discovers any new and useful process , machine, manufacture, or composition of matter, or any new and useful improvement thereof, may obtain a patent therefor, subject to the conditions and requirements of this title. (July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 797 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on Title 35, U.S.C., 1946 ed., § 31 (R.S. 4886, amended (1) Mar. 3, 1897, ch. 391, § 1, 29 Stat. 692 , (2) May 23, 1930, ch. 312, § 1, 46 Stat. 376 , (3) Aug. 5, 1939, ch. 450, § 1, 53 Stat. 1212 ). The corresponding section of existing statute is split into two sections, section 101 relating to the subject matter for which patents may be obtained, and section 102 defining statutory novelty and stating other conditions for patentability. Section 101 follows the wording of the existing statute as to the subject matter for patents, except that reference to plant patents has been omitted for incorporation in section 301 and the word “art” has been replaced by “ process ”, which is defined in section 100. The word “art” in the corresponding section of the existing statute has a different meaning than the same word as used in other places in the statute; it has been interpreted by the courts as being practically synonymous with process or method. “ Process ” has been used as its meaning is more readily grasped than “art” as interpreted, and the definition in section 100(b) makes it clear that “ process or method” is meant. The remainder of the definition clarifies the status of processes or methods which involve merely the new use of a known process , machine, manufacture, composition of matter, or material; they are processes or methods under the statute and may be patented provided the conditions for patentability are sa

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text explicitly excludes provisional applications, continuations, foreign counterparts, and other forms of patent rights from the definition of "Patent." This contradicts the general understanding and application of patent law, as reflected in 35 U.S.C. § 101, which states that "Whoever invents or discovers any new and useful process, machine, manufacture, or composition of matter, or any new and useful improvement thereof, may obtain a patent therefor". The modified text limits the scope of 'Patent', thereby misaligning with legally recognized definitions and potentially making the contract unenforceable. This falls under the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 1.1 "Affiliate" means any person or entity that controls, is controlled by or is under common control with the specified person or entity, but only so long as such control exists. The term "control" means the possession, directly or indirectly, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of an entity, whether through ownership of voting securities, by contract or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 "Affiliate" means any person or entity that controls, is controlled by or is under common control with the specified person or entity, but only so long as such control exists. The term "control" means the possession, directly or indirectly, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of an entity, exclusively through ownership of 100% of the voting securities, and explicitly not by contract or otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: The revised definition restricts "control" to only instances where one entity owns 100% of another entity's voting securities.  This conflicts with the common and legally accepted understanding of control, which includes control exerted through contractual agreements or other means of influence. By excluding 'contract or otherwise', it potentially allows entities with significant contractual control to evade affiliate status.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the definition of "control" as outlined in SEC Rule 12b-2.  The rule defines control as 'possession, direct or indirect, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise.' The modified definition limits control to only ownership, contradicting the SEC's broader definition and potentially impacting reporting requirements.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the established definition of 'control' by limiting it exclusively to ownership of voting securities, whereas the standard legal definition also includes control exerted through contractual agreements or other means, as indicated in the Law Explanation referencing SEC Rule 12b-2. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions '17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions', indicating the existence of a legal definition being contradicted by the changed contract language. The Perturbation Explanation also confirms that this deviates from legally accepted understandings of control.

perturbation.original_text: 1.26 "Sale," "Sell" or "Sold" mean the sale, transfer, exchange or other disposition of Products, by DIALOG or any of its Affiliates to any customer or other third party, directly or indirectly through one or more tiers of distribution, for consideration that is recognized as revenue by DIALOG or its Affiliates according to applicable generally accepted accounting principles.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.26 "Sale," "Sell" or "Sold" mean the transfer of Products, by DIALOG or any of its Affiliates to any customer or other third party, directly for consideration that is recognized as revenue by DIALOG or its Affiliates according to applicable generally accepted accounting principles.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'sale, exchange or other disposition' and 'indirectly through one or more tiers of distribution', the revised definition narrows the scope of what constitutes a "Sale."  This creates ambiguity by excluding exchanges (bartering, swapping etc) and sales through distributors or other intermediaries. In other words, the new definition ignores 'other forms of consideration' besides revenue.
perturbation.contradicted_Law: 26 U.S. Code § 1001 - Determination of amount of and recognition of gain or loss
perturbation.law_explanation: The change contradicts 26 U.S.C. § 1001, which governs the determination of gain or loss from the sale or other disposition of property. The exclusion of the term "other disposition" would exclude from royalty calculations any transaction that transfers ownership for something other than revenue, thereby potentially lowering the royalty fees paid to ENERGOUS.

Determination of amount of and recognition of gain or loss (a) Computation of gain or loss The gain from the sale or other disposition of property shall be the excess of the amount realized therefrom over the adjusted basis
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 1001 - Determination of amount of and recognition of gain or loss | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 1001 - Determination of amount of and recognition of gain or loss U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Computation of gain or loss The gain from the sale or other disposition of property shall be the excess of the amount realized therefrom over the adjusted basis provided in section 1011 for determining gain, and the loss shall be the excess of the adjusted basis provided in such section for determining loss over the amount realized. (b) Amount realized The amount realized from the sale or other disposition of property shall be the sum of any money received plus the fair market value of the property (other than money) received. In determining the amount realized— (1) there shall not be taken into account any amount received as reimbursement for real property taxes which are treated under section 164(d) as imposed on the purchaser, and (2) there shall be taken into account amounts representing real property taxes which are treated under section 164(d) as imposed on the taxpayer if such taxes are to be paid by the purchaser. (c) Recognition of gain or loss Except as otherwise provided in this subtitle, the entire amount of the gain or loss, determined under this section, on the sale or exchange of property shall be recognized. (d) Installment sales Nothing in this section shall be construed to prevent (in the case of property sold under contract providing for payment in installments) the taxation of that portion of any installment payment representing gain or profit in the year in which such payment is received. (e) Certain term interests (1) In general In determining gain or loss from the sale or other disposition of a term interest in property , that portion of the adjusted basis of such interest which is determined pursuant to section 1014, 1015, o
perturbation.justification: The modified text narrows the definition of 'Sale,' excluding 'exchange or other disposition' and 'indirectly through one or more tiers of distribution'. This contradicts 26 U.S.C. § 1001, which concerns gain or loss from the 'sale or other disposition of property'. By limiting the definition of 'Sale' to exclude 'other disposition,' the changed text potentially lowers royalty fees by excluding transactions that transfer ownership for something other than revenue. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both highlight that 26 U.S.C. § 1001 applies to the 'sale or other disposition of property.' The modified contract directly contradicts this by narrowing the definition, creating a legal contradiction by misaligning terminology with the law.
